comments.title: A Few Bad Apples?
comments.content: Hi JMG, there's currently an embarrassment of riches for spiritual practitioners seeking tools for their work. The Internet, and particularly the site Etsy, makes it effortless to find countless vendors offering magical equipment, altar items, all kinds of divination tools, every tarot and oracle deck you can imagine and much more. However, I see a pattern that gives me pause. An artist offering images of Athena and Thor will also sell depictions of Lucifer and Lilith. The company printing oracle decks will sell sets of cards representing the Goetic spirits. I can't see the creation of these items as neutral, it represents a focused intent to bring these powers into manifestation. Do you think it's best to avoid any products from vendors producing items dedicated to evil forces, or can they make this stuff without having it taint the rest of their merchandise by association?

replies.title: Re: A Few Bad Apples?
replies.content: To my mind, it's not a matter of tainting the merchandise, it's a matter of seriousness. I tend to think that those vendors who will provide anything and everything don't take any of it seriously -- they simply provide whatever they think will sell. That isn't an attitude that yields magically effective gear. Thus I shop from firms that take their work seriously enough to have some standards when it comes to what they will and won't produce.

There's a bevy of songs leveraging current political scandals, which could easily be underwritten by opposition parties looking to piggyback off Amelia's archetypal power.

As a counterbalance to all that ferocity and bitterness, the song "Sun All Year Round" celebrates that each of us has our homeland, where we know the foods, the weather, the people, and the culture — where we belong. The lyrics are a blessing and a wish for everyone to belong in their own homeland, rather than a curse born of misunderstanding and hatred. comments.title: Occult revival & second religiosity
comments.content:
Hi JMG!
As soon as I started listening to your podcasts mentioning Spengler everything happening nowadays makes so much sense. Do you think there will be another occult revival during the second religiosity or nah?
Also, what occult remnants, if any, do you think will “survive” into a new Spring centuries from now?

replies.title: Re: Occult revival & second religiosity
replies.content: Good heavens, yes. The Second Religiosity is always an attempt to stave off chaos and decline by going back to the religious forms of an earlier time, and it's especially attractive to intellectuals and the privileged classes. Meanwhile those people who are comfortable with chaos and oriented toward the future rather than the past tend to get involved instead with alternative spirituality and magic. It's no accident that while pop-culture Paganism and other forms of Magic Lite have been losing popularity of late, interest in serious occult practice hasn't been this high in a century. As for what will make it through the Winter phase, that's very hard to say in advance, but one thing's sure -- what survives will be what works.

Tell a bunch of people that they're personally responsible for everything bad that's ever happened to anyone because of their race and gender, and deny them any positive role models at all, and yeah, they're going to make a beeline for traditions that have such role models and offer them a path to redemption.

replies.title: Re: Occult revival & second religiosity
replies.content:
Hi JMG,

What does pop-culture Paganism look like? Who would be good examples of that approach?

What would be the opposite of pop-culture Paganism? Presumably, it would be something like 'serious' Paganism. Although I'm not sure what that might actually mean in practice.

Also, I'm not trying to play word games here, I'm genuinely curious about this.

Thanks in advance.

replies.title: Re: Occult revival & second religiosity
replies.content:
1) If you want to see pop-culture Paganism, go to TikTok and take in any randomly chosen 15 minutes or so of videos from teenage witches.

2) The alternative is serious Paganism. There's quite a lot of it out there. Like traditional Pagan faiths, it typically focuses on worshipping the gods and goddesses rather than casting spells, and it's usually highly literate (sometimes excessively so) in terms of surviving writings from Pagan societies, and very often also in the practices of those Pagan faiths that have survived to the present, such as Shinto. It's not especially mediagenic, and a lot of practitioners call themselves "polytheists" rather than "Pagans" because they don't want to be mistaken for the WitchTok brigade.

replies.title: Re: Occult revival & second religiosity
replies.content:
If you'd like to get a taste of some serious polytheists, a few options:

1) Our host's books on revival Druidry, such as The Druidry Handbook and his A World Full of Gods

2) Thomas Taylor's works on the Neo-Platonists and other translations (Taylor was not only a phenomenal translator, he worshipped the Greek Gods). Gregory Shaw is also worth checking out here.

3) If you prefer a more northern vibe, Galina Krasskova has written a lot from the Germanic perspective, including the book Devotional Polytheism, which aims to give a devotional approach to worshipping multiple Gods that's meant to be workable for any polytheist, but uses Germanic examples as what she is most familiar with. Personally, I think works better as "how to be a devotional heathen" than as a generic guide for worshippers of other Gods, but some of the attitudes and approaches are transferrable.

4) The Soul's Inner Statues by Kaye Boesme (available in its entirety for free here: https://kayeofswords.github.io/soulsinnerstatues/) comes from the point of view of a worshipper of the Greek Gods, but I think does a better job of being a "generic serious polytheistic default."

Anyhow, hope some of these are of interest.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Occult revival & second religiosity

replies.title: Re: Occult revival & second religiosity
replies.content: Unfortunately, I do not, so hopefully someone else will. My experience with Druidry has been more Welsh-focused.

replies.title: Re: Occult revival & second religiosity
replies.content:
The Druid Revival of the 18th and 19th centuries was entirely a concern of the Brythonic Celtic peoples -- the Welsh, the Cornish, and the Bretons; the Goidelic peoples -- the Irish, the Manx, and the Scots -- for some reason weren't interested in it at all. Thus Ireland came very late to the rebirth of Druidry. There are Druid groups there, and inevitably some of them have manufactured the usual sort of bogus origin stories dating themselves back to the birth of Cthulhu's grandmother, but all the evidence I've seen indicates that they were created in the 1970s if not more recently.

Mind you, there's a wealth of raw material for anyone interested in Old Irish polytheism, and these days there's a lively Irish Pagan scene in Ireland as well as a vast horde of wannabe Irish Druids in the US and elsewhere; most of the latter have no personal connection to Irish ethnicity or culture at all, and so there's a lot of pretense and sham, but there's also been some solid research. So you should be able to find raw material if you look.

replies.title: Re: Occult revival & second religiosity (these fragments I have shored against my ruins"
replies.content:
In a sense, "second religiosity" has been around for surprisingly quite a while, and has rather porous boundaries. TS Eliot would seem like a good example, and CS Lewis -- but I don't think Tolkien would be, since his Catholicism was inherited from his mother.

In the sense that it was a reaction against certain disenchanting aspects of the Enlightenment, Martinism (and similar developments) could be seen terms of Second Religiosity. Even earlier, Rosicrucian and Behmenist developments could be seen as reactions against the secularizing or rationalizing effects of the Reformation and Counter-Reformation.

Going back a bit earlier, though, there wasn't that much space between the dominant cultural currents and their esoteric forms. Occultism and esotericism as such had not yet become counter-cultural currents (though it has always been possible, in every society, to end up "doing it wrong". But it seems to me that second religiosity as a distinct current could only take off when primary religiosity, exoteric and esoteric, had pretty much would down.

LeGrand

replies.title: Re: Occult revival & second religiosity (these fragments I have shored against my ruins"
replies.content:
That's what Spengler says, certainly: the Second Religiosity only becomes an option as the age of reason winds down, and that comes after the original age of faith has waned. Ages of faith happen early in the history of every civilization, when religion is still a creative force, miracles and wonders abound, and mythic thought is the normal currency of the human intellect. In Spengler's terms, it's a phenomenon of springtime. The Second Religiosity is autumnal: formal, structured, defensive. It's the defensive perimeter that every civilization establishes as night closes in.

You're right about Eliot and Lewis, and the quote from The Waste Land is perfect: it's precisely the act of shoring the fragments of a failing civilization against the ruins of the old springtime faith that typifies the Second Religiosity. Eliot and Lewis were ahead of their time -- nor is it accidental that they're lionized these days by the proponents of the Second Religiosity.

replies.title: Re: Western Civilization's Yeats Phase
replies.content: The word "artificial" doesn't mean "far from reality." It literally means "the product of artifice" -- that is to say, planned, constructed, manufactured. After the 25th phase, a society loses the ability to plan, construct, and manufacture anything new or genuinely creative. Instead, it rehashes the past, borrows random bleeding chunks of material from other cultures, and increasingly becomes the plaything of unconscious forces and fantasies. If this doesn't seem familiar to you, I recommend looking out the window. ;-)

I'm not sure if your proviso about transhumanism is meant to clarify the purpose expressed by "deals with" or just aesthetically constrain what "sci-fi/fantasy" is supposed to refer to, but if it's the former, then "Radiant", the seventh book of James Alan Gardner's "League of Peoples" series, is the work of fiction that most resonates with my experience of such things, especially with respect to the states of subjective representational and decision-making tension they produce in lower beings (the viewpoint characters).

Gardner leaves enough hints for a reader to be able to guess where the strings are in his intended worldbuilding that connect the the surface-visible events to higher beings' wranglings, but the technological and ontological details of how that wrangling works don't seem that essential compared to the structural features of how the wrangling is motivated and what its typical outcomes are. This is like a version of that saying about "sufficiently advanced technology is indistinguishable from magic" that's inside-out from how Arthur C. Clarke ("I have seen far too many claims dissolve into thin air, far too many demonstrations exposed as fakes. It has been a long, and sometimes embarrassing, learning process") meant it.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
... A different way of saying part of that is to speculate that any sufficiently advanced technology, insofar as it maintains its purpose, ends up developing a whole ecosystem around meanings, intentions, and interpretations, and thus landing in a certain morass of copings (in the continental philosophy sense) about those things, which morass is the same one that magic starts out already hip-deep in. Because that's just what it means for something to maintain its purpose, in a space with a sufficiently wide range of (technologically newly provided) options.

This is reminiscent of Greenspun's rule, a half-joking maximum about software engineering, here emended as "Any sufficiently complicated software project not written in Common Lisp contains an ad hoc, informally-specified, bug-ridden, slow implementation of half of Common Lisp." (Lisp is a computer language built to enable automation of certain higher-level abstract tasks involving programs and parts of program states using a kind of narrow-purpose reflective self-representation; the claim is that people trying to put higher-level control structures in their software projects keep reinventing the features of Lisp.) We might, in a parallel way, guess that any sufficiently advanced technology that's not already a magical system from the bottom up, but still manages to maintain its purpose, contains an ad-hoc, informally-specified, bug-ridden, inefficient implementation of half of a magical system.

(there was this other reflection on the roles of purposiveness in alchemy vs. magic vs. science vs. engineering that was also relevant, but I don't have the link)

replies.title: Re: Candle Magic for 'Invisibility'
replies.content:
1) The best thing to do with the paper is bury it in your backyard if you have one; that makes the good fortune stay with you. The glass container should be washed thoroughly and then recycled, as a way of giving back.

2) Not for this purpose. Dark blue or indigo are specific for invisibility spells; black has other meanings and purposes.

3) That's correct. Day and hour is all you need. Technically speaking, in this kind of magic you're not invoking the planet, you're invoking its representative intelligence and spirit in the aura of the Earth.

replies.title: Re: Candle Magic for 'Invisibility'

replies.title: Re: Candle Magic for 'Invisibility'
replies.content:
That's a complicated question. If the Neoplatonists are right, we never contact a god directly -- we encounter one of the spirits or daimons who embody the influence of that god on what they called the psychic plane and modern occultists call the astral plane. This is why there's a family resemblance among thunder gods, say, but every thunder god is a little different; the god is the same, but the daimons who carry that god's influence to different cultures vary one from another.

The planetary intelligence is another channel for the influence of the corresponding god -- not a daimon, but a being of somewhat higher standing. Each planetary intelligence has a planetary spirit or daimon as his or her reflection on the psychic or astral plane. That is to say, it's a very complex cosmos!

comments.title: What if I am in the 8th Phase of "The Vision"?
comments.content:
Dear JMG

It occurred to me whilst studying The Vision that much that is written there and also by you about the 8th phase resembles parts of my life rather uncomfortably. Well into the mid years, this is a rather grueling realisation.

There have been questions in my life, such as "when does my life actually begin?" and then, when I have made some rather serious attempts to find my true path, it has been a road full of miseries, but also of great delights. Professionally I seem to do everything in the wrong order, with awful timing and awkward consequences. I have made moves in my life that make no sense to anyone but me, and not always to me either. It feels like reaching for those proverbial straws, as I very much feel like the gurgling sound of the gutter is about to swallow me any moment - or that it is constantly doing that.

It took me a good while to get diagnosed with some neurodivergent traits, such as probably a mild case of autism with a bunch of related issues. I accept it. This is how my executive function functions or fails to function. I trudge onwards, try to lead the best life I can, with the tools at my disposal, but at times it seems there is not much to show for it.

Yet I have managed to put past many addictions. The addictions themselves seem never to be the issues, but just some convenient slopes nearby when something else needs doing. The best thing I have in my life right now is magic and the regular practice of it. That truly is the straw of straws for me, the last hope I have, and I do cling to it at times with utter desperation. There have been results, positive ones even, and I plan on carrying on with the practices the rest of my life, no matter what. If for nothing else, then for having that one thing that I will not abandon, the one thing that keeps me, if not in the light, at least in the twilight.

Now I may be wrong about the 8th phase. Or partly wrong. Then I think it does not matter much, and instead consider the possibility that if the meditations on that phase help me attain a greater self knowledge, then it has served a purpose already. But a harsh phase it is, that is for sure.

Any advice?

replies.title: Re: What if I am in the 8th Phase of "The Vision"?
replies.content: The only useful advice I can offer is to keep clutching at those straws. That's what will build the strength that will catapult you out of Phase 8 into the following phase.

replies.title: Re: What if I am in the 8th Phase of "The Vision"?
replies.content:
My phase might be the opposite phase, 22. What will I, in this life or a future one, have to do to pass to Phase 23? If I get worn down, will the "amalgation" Yeats writes about naturally follow?

Patrick H

replies.title: Re: What if I am in the 8th Phase of "The Vision"?
replies.content: You need to pursue whatever form of intellectual or creative work appeals to you with all your strength, until it crumples beneath you and you come face to face with the true Mask, "Self-immolation." Only when you have worked up your capacities to the highest intensity of which they are capable will they free you from yourself and send you on into the fourth quarter.

replies.title: Re: What if I am in the 8th Phase of "The Vision"?
replies.content:
I approached that but I probably gave up top soon. Some of my enthusiasm for knowledge returned with your writings and Spengler's. I shouldn't expect that to happen if I had already passed the point of balance, right?

Patrick H

replies.title: Re: What if I am in the 8th Phase of "The Vision"?
replies.content: That's correct.

replies.title: Re: What if I am in the 8th Phase of "The Vision"?
replies.content:
So I should go the second mile with some of my intellectual interests, and I should achieve the goal of my phase. That seems doable with the Internet. Thank you.

Patrick H

replies.title: Re: What if I am in the 8th Phase of "The Vision"?
replies.content: Can the grasping-at-straws have the potential to actually work out well after OP has discovered his strength?

replies.title: Re: What if I am in the 8th Phase of "The Vision"?
replies.content: Yes, that's the whole point of it.

Good afternoon and thank you as always.

If it's not too complex to get into here, I'd be interested to know what sorts of astrological timing (presumably mostly centered around the movements of Jupiter) are worth paying attention to for working with money and building wealth, since I've heard you mention on podcasts that astrological timing has helped you maximize your success in publishing and the like. I have a fair amount of liquid assets lying around because my job pays well but I keep my expenses low, and I'd be interested in possibly making some investments with it (but I'm thinking more in terms of direct investments in promising small business and enterprises that do practical, real things, with less emphasis on the stock market and such). For whatever it's worth, my natal Jupiter is in Virgo, but is positively aspected to Mars and Venus.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You need to study your natal chart closely; a good delineation from a competent astrologer will help, but it will also benefit you to go over it yourself with the aid of any basic textbook, such as Julia and Derek Parker's The Compleat Astrologer. Pay very close attention to the 2nd, 5th, 8th, and 10th houses; these rule your income, speculation, your partner's income, and your career respectively. The planet ruling the sign on the cusp of each house rules that house in your chart, and you can tell by their condition, house placements, and aspects whether you will be fortunate or otherwise in that part of your life.

Those are your keys. When a transiting planet is applying to conjoin or favorably aspect a planet ruling one of those houses, that house will benefit. That's the time to act. The more fortunate the house ruler is in your natal chart, the better results you are likely to get. If you can get two or more well aspected -- for example, the Moon applying to conjoin your 10th house ruler while Mercury applies to sextile your 2nd house ruler -- it's time to go for broke. On the other hand, when planets apply to a negative aspect with a house ruler, hunker down and wait for a better opportunity to pursue something in that phase of your life.

replies.title: Re: Sophia
replies.content:
It's the Greek word for "wisdom," and in Gnosticism, well...
[IMAGE:0]
She's one of the Aeons, the timeless beings of the world of light of which our world of matter is a murky and jumbled reflection. In most versions of the classic Gnostic myth she is the last emanated of the Aeons, and fell from the world of light because she tried to create something according to her own will, rather than acting in harmony with the other Aeons. Her fall is what brought our world into being, and her redemption, according to the Gnostics, is the archetype of our redemption. Details? Every Gnostic denomination had its own ideas.

replies.title: Re: Gift for Mars
replies.content: I tend to rely on the standard correspondences, so in a case like yours I'd offer a knife or something else made of iron or steel. One custom you may not know about is the Celtic way of offering weapons to their warrior gods, whom the Romans all equated with Mars: they flung them, unsheathed, into a lake or river. (The whole business about Excalibur coming from the lake and returning to it has very ancient roots. I've done this with excellent results. Buy a sword, dedicate it to Mars, go to a place where the water's good and deep, and fling it, tumbling and flashing, through the air with all your strength so it plunges into deep water: that's a grand way of making an offering and will be appreciated by Mars in any of his aspects.

replies.title: Re: Improving Phrasing
replies.content:
I tend to think of this world as only resembling a world that's a non-"living-body" (in your sense), and only maintaining that resemblance, in order to serve a larger, unseen world's didactic (and/or artistic) purposes, teaching some unclear population of souls or soul-parts (re the relations between which, compare Ronald Coase's essay "The Nature of the Firm") about the habits and virtues appropriate to worlds with blindly impersonal mechanical-mathematical laws. Perhaps the unseen world prioritizes these lessons because the unseen world is actually built on top of a world that really was blindly impersonal, and someone needs to stay in contact with reality for the whole system to stay afloat. Or perhaps the unseen world prioritizes these lessons because blind impersonality is a naturally recurring pattern of phenomenon in the unseen world, similar to how bureaucratic ossification works, or the how consolidation of the rules of a vague language into a precise language works once enough communities of usage start to depend on that language. Whatever the reason, the "strings" would be just the faculties that the true world had had all along; it was merely restraining itself from using them.

I spelled out a slightly more specific theory of this kind of observer's-interpretation-and-perception-relative synchronicity at https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/300859.html?thread=51822651#cmt51822651 .

Alternative theory: this universe is continuous with one that was originally non-"living" in the past, but as we experience the universe now, it's the result of a bunch of wrangling by intelligent beings with sufficiently advanced technology, but they all nonetheless have some reason not to deviate too far from the appearance of how things would go if it had been non-living. Cf. https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/359082.html?thread=59245738#cmt59279018 and https://ecosophia.dreamwidth.org/360870.html?thread=59400102#cmt59434918 .

I don't really expect that it will be possible to get a closer picture of "how" in this life, other than a sort of by-courtesy observer-relative "how-for-you".

comments.title: On the Nature Spirits in Maine
comments.content:
Greetings Mr. Greer,
I wanted to get your and your reader's thoughts again on an experience I had recently with the nature spirits in Maine.
More than a year ago, I inquired if you had ever traveled to Maine and experienced the nature spirits there and had anything to comment. You hadn't, but I was grateful for the reply.
Well, it may be of you and your reader's interest that I have recently.
What I found was that the whole state, or at least the part of it south of Lake Moosehead and likely down to Portland, is covered with some kind of spiritual blockade. I've spent the time since my first post in spiritual training, making my perceptions and ability to make contact with entities stronger, but when I arrived, I couldn't get ahold of either of the deities I normally work with. Maine has always had a curiously anemic religious and occult scene (lots of reports of spiritual activity from non-humans, but very little from people). Even Christian evangelicalism and recently arrived Islam seems to get smothered in the crib. They don't take off in nearly the way you would expect.
A friend suggested I try to get a hold of a couple of European forest deities, like Cernunnos. I tried that, and what I saw was the vision of a horned humanoid with a shadowy face who looked like Cernunnos, but wasn't. As I was walking through the woods on some family property, I found two plastic bucks washed up in the delta of a river and frozen into the snow and ground. I pulled them up, and declared aloud that I was offering this to the forest as a token of my friendship, and took them to the trash. The next day or after, the block was largely lifted and I could make some contact with my deities again, but I got the vibe that the access was almost grudging. It wasn't outright hostility, but a very, very cold shoulder.
Tucker Carlson, among one prominent resident of the state, has talked about the outright hostility he has experienced in the woods there, the sense that humans aren't wanted. I agree, but also there are large areas where the strategy appears to be simply slowly strangle human life by blockade. By most metrics, it's working.

replies.title: Re: On the Nature Spirits in Maine
replies.content: Fascinating. I've felt something similar in the forests of western Washington state. The forests there are conifers, and very dark -- when the skies are cloudy they look black. There a very strong sense that if you stray off the trail you will probably never be found -- and yes, this happens quite often. Interestingly, the native peoples had the same sense. They built their villages on the seashore or on river banks, never back in the woods, and told eerie stories about the skookums (powerful spiritual beings) that lived in the woods and would grab the unwary.

The month kicks off with a spectacular planetary alignment as Mercury, Venus, Saturn, Jupiter, Uranus, and Neptune gather across the evening sky. This close encounter will be observable from locations around the world, while in Antarctica and select parts of the Indian Ocean—including Madagascar and Mauritius—the Moon will fully occult Antares, briefly hiding the star from view.

The Gamma Normids meteor shower reaches its peak around March 14–15. Though a modest display, it offers its best viewing from the Southern Hemisphere, and the thin waning crescent Moon will provide darker skies to help any faint meteors stand out.

March 20 brings the vernal equinox, when day and night balance almost perfectly at around 12 hours each as the Sun crosses the celestial equator into the northern hemisphere.

Throughout the entire month, aurora activity has a good chance of intensifying. comments.title: Meditation 7 questions
comments.content:
I recall you describing a method for discursive meditation to spur the session along when you're stuck. It involved seven questions and seven processes, or something similar.
'Who' 'what' 'when' 'where'
Was paired with:
'Being' 'becoming' etc.

I can't remember many more details, other than it sounded like a good way to examine an idea from all angles. Would you be willing to share it again?

replies.title: Re: Meditation 7 questions
replies.content:
It's called the sevenfold wheel of thought. There are seven concepts and seven questions, and you combine them.

Concepts:
Being
Becoming
Source
Substance
Power
Purpose
Value

Questions:
Whether?
What?
How?
Who?
Where?
When?
Why?

You combine each of the questions with each of the concepts, thus...

Whether it is?
What is it?
How is it?
Who (or whose) is it?
Where is it?
When is it?
Why is it?
Whether it becomes?
